data.title: Nike extends partnership with LSU, launches NIL program with 10 LSU athletes
data.description:
Nike (NKE) is extending its five-decade partnership with Louisiana State University ((LSU)) sports, outfitting the college’s athletes with uniforms and gear until at least 2036.

The company also announced the launch of its “Blue Ribbon Elite” Name, Image, and Likeness program at LSU -- known more commonly as NIL -- representing 10 athletes from various LSU sports programs.

“The athlete-centered approach takes shape for the first time at LSU through the addition of 10 top Tigers athletes to Nike’s NIL roster, illustrating that for Nike, it’s not just about outfitting the best – it’s about creating with the boldest,” Nike said in a statement.

Through its NIL program, these 10 athletes representing LSU football, baseball, basketball, volleyball, and gymnastics, will collaborate with Nike (NKE) on product development and content creation, and participate in Nike (NKE) brand campaigns similar to Alabama wide receiver Ryan Williams’ DICK’s Sporting Goods (DKS) campaign and USC and UCLA athletes featured in NikeSKIMS ads.

The NIL program “is about more than gear,” said Nike’s head of Global Sports Marketing. “It’s about collaboration, creativity, and meaningful impact, giving athletes a platform to influence product, innovation, storytelling and culture.”

Nike shares are up more than 1% on Thursday, flirting with resistance at its 200-day moving average.

data.title: Ciena outlines $5.7B-$6.1B fiscal 2026 revenue target with 24% growth driven by AI demand
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Ciena Corporation (CIEN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Gary Smith announced that Ciena reported record fiscal fourth quarter and full year revenue of $1.35 billion and $4.77 billion, respectively, attributing this to “sustained, purposeful investment and focus on leading high-speed connectivity technologies, together with disciplined execution and deep collaboration with our customers.” He highlighted Q4 earnings per share of $0.91, a 69% year-over-year increase, and a full-year EPS of $2.64, up 45% from the prior year. Smith also noted record orders of $7.8 billion for the year, leading to a record backlog entering 2026.

  • Smith emphasized accelerating demand from cloud customer providers, including large hyperscalers and emerging neoscalers, stating, “Orders from cloud providers are very strong, and ramping across our portfolio and they constitute a substantial portion of our growing backlog.” He mentioned that service provider orders were up nearly 70% for the year and that the top 3 service providers’ revenue grew 16% from 2024 to 2025. Smith reported that Ciena’s optical market share increased by 2 points year-to-date, and he expects further gains in 2026.

  • Smith described strategic wins including a large MOFN project in India, multiple major MOFN wins in new geographies, and a threefold growth in “in and around the data center opportunities” from 2024 to 2025. He highlighted surpassing the target for pluggable revenue, reaching more than $168 million in FY 2025, and initial shipments of WaveLogic 6 Nano 800-gig pluggables.

  • On acquisitions, Smith noted the completion of the Nubis Communications purchase, stating that “Nubis’ technologies and expertise will help us address the scale-up and scale-out opportunities inside the data center.”

  • CFO Marc Graff stated, “Our Q4 gross margin sequentially improved and exceeded [indiscernible] our guide by 90 basis points. This was largely due to higher revenue and software mix.” Graff also reported, “We left the year with $1.4 billion in cash after generating $371 million in cash from operations in Q4 and free cash flow of $326 million.”

Outlook

  • Ciena updated its fiscal 2026 revenue guidance to a range of $5.7 billion to $6.1 billion, reflecting nearly 24% annual growth at the midpoint versus the 17% growth rate previously discussed in September.

  • Graff stated, “We continue to expect gross margins for fiscal '26 to be in the range of 43%, plus or minus 1 point.” He further noted, “We expect adjusted operating expense in fiscal '26 to be flat at approximately $1.52 billion after accounting for the Nubis operating expenses post acquisition.”

  • Ciena now anticipates fiscal 2026 operating margins to improve to 17%, plus or minus 1 point, with capital expenditures projected between $250 million and $275 million.

  • For Q1 2026, revenue is expected in the range of $1.35 billion to $1.43 billion, adjusted gross margin between 43% and 44%, and adjusted operating expenses of approximately $380 million.

Financial Results

  • Q4 revenue was $1.35 billion, up 20% year-over-year, and $70 million above the midpoint of guidance. Full-year revenue reached $4.77 billion, up 19% year-over-year.

  • The Optical business grew 19% year-over-year, with RLS revenue up 72%. The Routing and Switching business increased 49% year-over-year, and Global Services grew 25%. Blue Planet achieved $34 million in revenue in Q4 and $115 million for the year, achieving full-year profitability.

  • Adjusted gross margin for Q4 was 43.4%; for the year, it was 42.7%. Q4 adjusted operating expense was approximately $409 million and $1.51 billion for the year. Q4 adjusted operating margin was 13.2%, operating margin for the year reached 11.2%. Annual adjusted EPS was $2.64.

  • Ciena exited the year with about $5 billion of backlog, with $3.8 billion in hardware and software.

Q&A

  • Ruben Roy, Stifel: Asked how new scale across and DCOM opportunities are reflected in guidance and for details on hyperscaler wins. Marc Graff: Confirmed that all new opportunities are included in the guidance and that the “in and around data center” revenue portion is increasing to low double digits in 2026’s guide. Gary Smith: Explained, “We’ve got 3 out of the 4 hyperscalers have selected us for their scale across training models…they are clearly hundreds of millions each but they are different in terms of their scale at this stage.”

  • Simon Leopold, Raymond James: Inquired about the timeline for new hyperscaler projects and customer breakdown. Gary Smith: Stated, “I would expect us to take revenue for all 3 of these hyperscalers in '26 or begin to take revenue in '26… the ramp on this as we get to '27 and through '28.” Marc Graff: Specified AT&T as one of the three 10% revenue customers in Q4.

  • Atif Malik, Citi: Asked about Nubis adoption and gross margin drivers. Gary Smith: Noted “accelerated interest now that they’re part of a broader company.” Scott McFeely: Clarified Nubis’ portfolio opportunities start in ’26 and ’27. Marc Graff: Identified ramping 800-gig pluggables and customer pricing discussions as margin drivers.

  • George Notter, Wolfe Research: Sought clarity on supply chain constraints. Marc Graff: Highlighted constraints mainly in photonics and optical parts, with increased CapEx to support demand. Scott McFeely: Added that Ciena’s early view and vertical integration have been advantages.

  • Tal Liani, BofA: Questioned the visibility into growth and margin cyclicality. Marc Graff: Asserted high near-term visibility, stating, “We’re essentially sold out… we’ve got really good visibility of what the next several quarters look like just because we’ve got those orders in place.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for details on growth visibility, hyperscaler wins, scale across timing, supply constraints, and margin sustainability, signaling a mix of curiosity and skepticism, particularly about the durability of demand and margin expansion.

  • Management maintained a confident tone during prepared remarks, using phrases such as “absolute conviction” and “increasing confidence that the durability of demand and our business and financial trajectory are very strong.” During Q&A, management responded directly, provided granular detail, and acknowledged headwinds, but continued to express optimism.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone shifted from cautious optimism to robust confidence, while analysts increased their focus on forward visibility and margin drivers.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Fiscal 2026 revenue growth guidance increased from 17% to nearly 24% at the midpoint, a notable revision from last quarter.

  • Management’s confidence has visibly strengthened, with explicit references to record orders, accelerating demand, and market share gains.

  • Analysts’ questions shifted from general demand and portfolio strategy last quarter to seeking detail on specific hyperscaler wins, timing, and margin drivers in the current call.

  • Key metrics such as revenue, EPS, backlog, and operating margin all showed sequential and year-over-year improvement.

  • Strategic focus has intensified on AI-driven opportunities, cloud provider wins, and rapid scaling of “in and around data center” solutions, with increased capital allocation to support these priorities.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged supply chain constraints, particularly in photonics and optical components, and detailed proactive capacity expansions and supplier negotiations as mitigations.

  • Rising input costs and ramping new product introductions were cited as headwinds to gross margin, with expectations for improvement as scale and pricing actions take effect in late fiscal 2026.

  • Analysts noted potential risks in supply chain, margin cyclicality, and the timing of large hyperscaler projects.

Final Takeaway

Ciena’s fourth quarter call highlighted record revenue, robust EPS growth, and an unprecedented order backlog, underpinned by accelerating AI-driven demand from both cloud and service provider customers. The company raised its fiscal 2026 revenue growth target to nearly 24% and expects further operating margin expansion, supported by major wins with hyperscalers, expanded data center solutions, and ongoing strategic investments. Management projects continued market leadership and durable demand, while actively addressing supply chain and margin risks to capitalize on the massive network investments required for the AI era.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Paramount is now the frontrunner to buy Warner Bros. on prediction market Polymarket
data.description:
According to prediction market Polymarket, Paramount Skydance (PSKY) now has higher odds of acquiring Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD) than Netflix (NFLX).

A high-stakes battle between Paramount (PSKY) and Netflix (NFLX) over Warner (WBD) is set to unfold after the David Ellison-led firm launched a hostile bid for the entire company on Monday. That move came after Netflix (NFLX) last week said it would buy Warner's (WBD) studio and streaming assets in a deal with an enterprise value of $82.7B.

See Polymarket's update below:

Paramount (PSKY) top boss Ellison on Wednesday sent a lengthy letter to Warner's (WBD) shareholders, trying to convince them that his $108B total buyout proposal was better than Netflix's (NFLX) offer. 

Bloomberg News on Wednesday said the bidding war between PSKY and NFLX would stretch into next year, citing people familiar with WBD's thinking.

According to rival prediction market Kalshi, the odds of Paramount (PSKY) buying Warner (WBD) before December 2026 are at 51%.

data.title: Lilly rises on “best-in-class” data for new weight loss therapy
data.description:
Eli Lilly (LLY) shares spiked ~3% on Thursday after the obesity drugmaker posted Phase 3 data showing its next-gen therapy retatrutide can generate up to 24% of weight loss, a “best-in-class" efficacy according to analysts.

Citing 68-week data, Indiana-based LLY said in the premarket that its Phase 3 TRIUMPH-4 succeeded, as retatrutide at the highest dose caused up to 24% weight loss while reducing a clinical score related to osteoarthritic pain.

The GIP, GLP-1, and glucagon triple hormone receptor agonist has delivered “best-in-class weight loss data,” Edmund Ingham, Seeking Alpha Investing Group Leader for Haggerston BioHealth, wrote, reiterating his Buy rating on LLY.

LLY’s “Zepbound achieved mean average weight loss in patients of >20%—a best-in-class result at the time—since equaled by Novo Nordisk's (NVO) high-dose Wegovy,” Ingham wrote, referring to existing FDA-approved obesity therapies.

“The results have exceeded analysts’ expectations (who had hoped for >20% weight loss, which retatrutide easily exceeded),” he added.

“Though the 68-week study is for osteoarthritis of the knee rather than obesity-focused, we suspect the weight loss efficacy above our 25% expectations will be well-received by investors,” Citi analyst Geoff Meacham, who has a Buy rating on LLY, added, according to Bloomberg.

“With relatively clear safety, these results help to solidify retatrutide’s profile as an even higher efficacy next-generation GLP-1+ asset,” Evan David Seigerman, an analyst at BMO Capital Markets, wrote, with an Outperform rating on LLY.

The results met “the high bar set by investors into the readout,” Seigerman said, while J. P. Morgan’s Chris Schott downplayed safety concerns after as many as 18% of participants who received retatrutide at the highest dose dropped out of TRIUMPH-4 due to adverse events.

Despite a tolerability profile that appeared worse than Zepbound's, the rates of gastrointestinal side effects were not all that high compared to the broader GLP-1 class of obesity drugs, Schott, who has an Overweight recommendation on LLY, added.

data.title: Fed terminates 2024 JPMorgan order, 2014 and 2020 Credit Suisse actions
data.description:
The Federal Reserve said Thursday it has terminated three enforcement actions, closing two legacy orders against UBS (UBS)-owned Credit Suisse and a 2024 order against JPMorgan Chase (JPM).

In last year's March, JPMorgan (JPM) was fined $348M by the Fed and the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency for inadequately monitoring firm and client trading activities for market misconduct.

For Credit Suisse, the 2014 cease-and-desist order required the company to fix oversight and controls after its tax-evasion case. And the 2020 order mandated broad improvements to its U.S. risk-management and anti-money laundering compliance.

data.title: Zealand inks deal with Chinese pharma to develop oral drugs for metabolic diseases
data.description:
• Zealand Pharma (ZLDPF) has signed a licensing and collaboration agreement with Shanghai-based OTR Therapeutics to develop new therapies for metabolic diseases. 
  • Terms call for OTR to receive an upfront payment $20M (could rise to $30M under certain conditions) and potential milestone payments of  ~$2.5B, plus tiered royalties.
  • Zealand has responsibility for development, regulatory submissions, and commercialization globally. 
  • The Danish pharma will leverage OTR's proprietary small-molecule platform.
  • Zealand's own obesity candidates are large-molecule biologics. In phase 3, it has the glucagon/GLP-1 receptor dual agonist survodutide in partnership with Boehringer Ingelheim, while in phase 2, it has petrelintide, an amylin analog, in development with Roche (RHHBY).

data.title: Nordson anticipates 6%–12% adjusted EPS growth for 2026 as backlog rises 5%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Nordson Corporation (NDSN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Sundaram Nagarajan, President and CEO, highlighted that "sales were up 1% over prior year, inclusive of the divestiture of our medical contract manufacturing business that closed on September 2." He reported "adjusted earnings per share grew 9% over the prior year, reaching the high end of our fourth quarter guidance," and noted a record EBITDA of $256 million and EBITDA margin of 34% in the quarter. Nagarajan also emphasized that "we achieved record sales of $2.8 billion, up 4% from last year," and "delivered record adjusted earnings per share of $10.24, exceeding the midpoint of our initial full year guidance."

  • Nagarajan pointed to the successful integration of Atrion Medical, stating it "contributed nicely to sales and EPS growth in the first year," and underscored strategic portfolio actions, including "the divestiture of our contract manufacturing business, driving immediate improvement in our margins and increased focus on our remaining differentiated medical businesses."

  • Daniel Hopgood, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "Fourth quarter 2025 sales were $752 million, up 1% compared to the prior year's fourth quarter sales of $744 million." He noted "adjusted operating profit increased 6% year-over-year to $218 million," with EBITDA up 6% to $256 million or 34% of sales. Hopgood added, "interest expense improved $4 million year-over-year, driven by reduced leverage and a stable to declining rate environment," and reported a tax expense of $31 million, for an effective tax rate of 17.1% in the quarter.

  • Hopgood detailed that "GAAP net income totaled $152 million or $2.69 per diluted share," while "adjusted earnings per share totaled $3.03 per share, a 9% increase over the prior year and $0.08 above the midpoint of our quarterly guidance." He called it "a quarterly record for the company."

Outlook

  • Nagarajan shared that for fiscal 2026, Nordson enters "with approximately $600 million in backlog, up 5% from the prior year-end, excluding backlog associated with the divested business." The company expects to deliver "full year sales in the range of 1% to 6% above fiscal 2025 sales."

  • Management forecasted "full year 2026 adjusted earnings...in the range of 6% to 12% growth per diluted share, with a midpoint of 9% adjusted earnings growth." For Q1 fiscal 2026, sales are forecasted in the range of $630 million to $670 million and adjusted earnings in the range of $2.25 to $2.45 per diluted share.

  • Hopgood stated, "For modeling purposes, in fiscal 2026, assume an estimated effective tax rate of 18.5% to 19.5%, capital expenditures of approximately $55 million to $65 million, and interest expense of approximately $85 million to $95 million."

Financial Results

  • Hopgood reported that "Medical and Fluid Solutions sales of $220 million increased 10% compared to the prior year's fourth quarter...organic sales volume was up nicely at 7%." The segment achieved EBITDA for the quarter of $88 million or 40% of sales—a 21% increase over the prior year.

  • The Industrial Precision Solutions segment registered sales of $362 million, a decrease of 2% compared to the prior year, with organic sales down just under 4%. Meanwhile, Advanced Technology Solutions sales were $171 million, down 4% compared to the prior year, largely due to "weakness in x-ray systems demand."

  • Nordson's full year free cash flow conversion was 136% of net income, enabling "repurchase of about $300 million in shares, increased dividends for the 62nd consecutive year and reduced our net debt, ending the year at a 2.1x leverage ratio."

Q&A

  • Michael Halloran, Robert W. Baird & Co., asked about broadening semiconductor applications in ATS. Nagarajan responded that strength remains in semiconductor space, especially for "AI applications, cloud computing and such," with automotive stabilizing and general electronics showing "lower growth...when compared to the semiconductor growth rates."

  • Halloran inquired about margin trajectory. Hopgood replied, "On the Medical side, we had a really strong quarter. I don't know that 40% plus is the right way to think about it. I think we're very comfortable maintaining the upper 30s in our Medical business."

  • Mitchell Moore, KeyBanc, questioned expectations for polymer processing. Nagarajan replied, "We're at the bottom and our expectation is, going into the year, that things improve from where we are, certainly not getting any more difficult than being a drag on IPS."

  • Matt Summerville, D.A. Davidson, asked about x-ray inspection. Nagarajan explained, "There is a transition in technology that is happening in testing as we speak. We have some new technologies that we are testing right now, which certainly will have an impact, but probably not in '26, probably in '27."

  • Andrew Buscaglia, BNP Paribas, asked about guidance conservatism. Hopgood replied, "We think it's prudent to plan for kind of our midpoint guidance. And on the downside, I would say that contemplates something happens this year."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on underlying demand, margin sustainability, and guidance conservatism, with a tone that was largely neutral, occasionally slightly skeptical, particularly around growth breadth and guidance ranges.

  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing operational achievements and strategic execution. During Q&A, responses were measured and at times defensive, with phrases like "I would say..." and "we think it's prudent." The tone remained consistent with the previous quarter, where management was similarly confident but cautious given macro uncertainty.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw a shift from a heavier focus on double-digit ATS growth in Q3 to a more balanced portfolio view, with improvements in Medical and a stable IPS outlook. The divestiture of the medical contract manufacturing business was completed, directly impacting portfolio mix and margins.

  • Compared to Q3, backlog is now up 5% year-over-year, and free cash flow conversion remains strong, though sequential cash flow decreased as is typical seasonally. Management's tone has become slightly more optimistic on end-market stabilization, especially in polymer processing and automotive, both cited as having "troughed."

  • Analysts in both quarters focused on order trends, margin sustainability, and segment-level growth, but Q4 included more probing on guidance ranges and risk management.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited macroeconomic uncertainty and potential downside scenarios as reasons for maintaining guidance ranges. Hopgood explained, "we think it's still important to make sure that we're...prepared for any potential outcome in the marketplace."

  • Segment-level risks include continued softness in ATS x-ray systems and potential delays in polymer processing and automotive recoveries.

  • Analysts questioned whether guidance reflects sufficient optimism given apparent stabilization in key end markets, and probed for details on segment-level order trends.

Final Takeaway

Nordson Corporation enters fiscal 2026 with a strengthened portfolio, record cash generation, and a 5% higher backlog compared to last year. Leadership expects 1%–6% sales growth and 6%–12% adjusted EPS growth for the upcoming year, supported by operational discipline, a focused M&A strategy, and signs of stabilization across key segments. Management remains confident in the company's ability to drive profitable growth and return value to shareholders, while maintaining a balanced approach to capital allocation and risk management.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Culp outlines $20M annual cost savings target as restructuring nears completion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Culp, Inc. (CULP) Q2 2026

Management View

  • Robert Culp, President and CEO, opened the call highlighting continued efforts to improve operating performance despite "challenging macro conditions" in the home furnishings sector, noting, "the tide generally remains out for housing and related furniture purchases, and we are improving our business gradually and in spite of these conditions."
  • Management emphasized progress on two strategies: winning market share and optimizing the cost structure. Culp stated, "with the adjustments we've made to optimize our platform...we have the capacity to absorb additional production driven by any uptick in demand without the need to spend significant capital dollars."
  • The CEO emphasized market share gains, particularly in the bedding segment, mentioning "sequentially increase our overall sales in the second quarter despite having 1 less week than the first quarter and to increase sales in our bedding segment, both sequentially and year-over-year."
  • Culp outlined the benefits of a multi-location manufacturing strategy, which provides flexibility amid tariff changes, stating, "the winners in a fluid trade environment are very likely to be companies that can give customers multiple geographic manufacturing options to better navigate tariff impacts."
  • The company completed a comprehensive restructuring project, consolidating its North American bedding operations, reducing the bedding workforce by almost 35%, and rationalizing its upholstery finishing operation in China. Culp reported, "we continue to expect approximately $11 million in annualized cost savings and efficiency gains from this project."
  • Project Blaze integrated the mattress and upholstery divisions, with cost savings and efficiency gains of approximately $3.5 million anticipated from operational consolidations.
  • Recent price adjustments intend to address tariff uncertainty, expected to generate about $2.5 million in annualized margin improvement in the bedding segment.
  • Culp noted ongoing initiatives to reduce the facility footprint in China and further SG&A reductions.
  • "Starting with our restructuring project in fiscal '25 and continuing through the completion of these other initiatives...we expect to enter fiscal '27 with a benefit of over $20 million in annualized cost savings and enhancements going forward," said Culp.
  • CFO Kenneth Bowling reported, "Consolidated net sales for the second quarter were $53.2 million, a sequential improvement from the first quarter sales of $50.7 million...Consolidated gross profit for the quarter was $5.8 million or 10.9% of sales."
  • Bowling also indicated, "SG&A expense for the quarter was $8.7 million, an approximate 7% improvement compared with SG&A expense for the prior year period, reflecting cost savings from our restructuring initiatives."

Outlook

  • Culp provided limited forward guidance, citing ongoing market and tariff uncertainty. "We currently expect steady consolidated sales performance in the third quarter and throughout the remainder of fiscal '26 with higher expectations for the bedding segment."
  • Management expects "improving gross profit and lower SG&A, resulting in continued significant improvement in operating loss and near breakeven to positive adjusted EBITDA for the third quarter."
  • The company anticipates a $4.7 million cash inflow in the fourth quarter from the sale of the Canada facility, potentially arriving in the third quarter.

Financial Results

  • Consolidated net sales for Q2 were $53.2 million. Gross profit was $5.8 million, or 10.9% of sales. Adjusted consolidated gross profit was $6.7 million, or 12.6% of sales.
  • SG&A expense for Q2 was $8.7 million. Loss from operations was $3.5 million, with an adjusted operating loss of $2 million. Adjusted EBITDA was negative $1 million.
  • Bedding segment sales were $30.8 million, with gross profit of $3.1 million or 10.1% of sales. Upholstery segment sales were $22.4 million, with gross profit of $3.6 million or 16.1% of sales.
  • Total cash was $10.7 million and outstanding debt was $18.3 million at quarter-end.

Q&A

  • Douglas Lane, Water Tower Research LLC, asked about the realization of $20 million in cost savings and the timing. Robert Culp responded, “by the time we get to Q4, we would expect to have majority of everything done, and we would have as clean of pictures we have...from a cost standpoint.”
  • Lane inquired about the incremental margin on future sales growth. CFO Bowling answered, "we're going to be able to keep a significant amount of those incremental dollars as we grow the business based on the platform we have today."
  • Lane questioned the impact and timeline of new tariffs from Turkey and Haiti. Culp answered, "Turkey...went from a 10% to a 15%...it could take us 60 days with a customer or with a strategy to adjust that....Haiti...we've gone from 0 tariff to 15% overnight. So we have to adjust that. And we will adjust it and believe it's -- we can easily adjust it, but not as quick as the pain."
  • Lane followed up on tariff volatility. Culp replied, "I want to believe it's starting to settle...we've become very proficient on managing tariff change and we have ways to mitigate it."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were cautiously optimistic, focusing on the realization of cost savings and the ability to mitigate tariff impacts while expressing interest in future incremental margin potential.
  • Management maintained a confident but cautious tone, repeatedly emphasizing adaptability and ongoing cost discipline. Direct quotes included, "we are positioning ourselves to do whatever it takes to adjust to the demand cycle" and "we are very -- fiscal '27 will be a very clean position heading into the market."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts continued to probe on cost realization and market recovery timing, while management sustained a pragmatic and proactive tone, with slightly greater confidence in the restructuring nearing completion.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remained limited; the company still refrains from providing detailed forecasts due to market uncertainty.
  • Strategic focus intensified on cost optimization, with an increased total cost savings target ($20 million annualized) compared to $18 million discussed previously.
  • Key metrics show modest sequential sales improvement, bedding segment sales growth, and continued improvement in adjusted operating loss.
  • Management tone reflects increased confidence in the restructuring's impact, while analyst sentiment is steady, focusing on execution and external risks.
  • Both quarters highlighted tariff management, but Q2 featured more detail on new tariffs and ongoing mitigation strategies.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted ongoing "challenging macro conditions" and "acute pressure on housing affordability," which continues to suppress unit sales.
  • Tariff volatility remains a risk, with recent increases from Turkey and Haiti requiring "at least a 60-day lag" to pass on costs.
  • Inventory management and non-cash impairments were recognized as part of ongoing warehouse consolidation.
  • The company is still exposed to weak consumer sentiment, especially in the upholstery business.
  • Management's mitigation strategies include aggressive cost rationalization, strategic price adjustments, and a flexible global production footprint.

Final Takeaway

Culp’s leadership underscored that the company is in the final stages of a comprehensive transformation, targeting over $20 million in annualized cost savings as it enters fiscal 2027. The ongoing efforts to streamline operations, integrate divisions, and proactively manage tariffs and costs position Culp to achieve profitability and capitalize on any future market upturn without the need for significant new investment. Management remains focused on maintaining liquidity, completing restructuring actions, and adjusting to market demand, with a clear message that Culp is prepared to respond to changing industry conditions and deliver improved performance as the environment stabilizes.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: SEC's new approach reduces guardrails for individual investors, Commissioner Crenshaw says
data.description:
U.S. Securities and Exchange Commissioner Caroline Crenshaw listed a raft of criticisms about recent changes she has seen at the SEC that disadvantages retail investors, she said on Thursday in her last public speech before her term on the SEC ends. Crenshaw, the lone Democrat on the SEC, was sworn in as a commissioner on Aug. 17, 2020.

She pointed to the SEC's moves to recommend requiring public company financial disclosures twice a year, instead of four times a year; the rule that will allow retail investors to include private assets and crypto in retirement accounts; and increased barriers for investors to submit proposals for publicly traded companies' proxies.

"It's unsettling to see how precipitously the commission" has been willing to undo guardrails, Crenshaw said at a Brookings Institution event on the Future of Financial Regulation. "We're seeing a trend toward devaluing investors' rights," she said, describing recent rule changes as showing "outright hostility to investor proxy proposals."

The argument for limiting investor influence is it will revitalize the market for IPOs, but IPOs can only be revitalized by investor money, Crenshaw said. She accused the current commission of "moving markets out of the light and into the darkness."

Furthermore, much of the policymaking is occurring behind closed doors, she contended. "This commission is enshrouding policymaking in darkness," Crenshaw said.

The SEC is implementing much of its new vision through staff statements. "This approach flouts notice-and-comment rulemaking," she said. There have been no proposals going through the notice-and-comment process since the start of the second Trump administration, she noted.

This shift means that regulators lose out on the public flagging blind spots. At the same time, the public loses out in hearing the voices that are behind policymaking, she added.

The SEC's changes to rules regarding investor influence have treated investors like "silly children," she said. "Investors deserve better."

The looser regulations allowing retail investors access to private markets expose the public to risky investments, Crenshaw contended. "It's risky and it's reckless."

Private investments don't have the same guardrails as investments in public markets. Retail investors will likely be "kept in the dark about fees and expenses. Retail investors will almost certainly be subject to higher fees than larger investors," she said.

At the same time, enforcement actions have been rarer, and perpetrators of fraud have been pardoned or had enforcement actions dropped. "Whistleblower awards have all but ground to a halt," she noted.

When tools to detect fraud are eliminated and the cost of fraud drops, "fraud will occur," she warned. To that point, SEC staff numbers, across the aboard, have dropped 15%-20%, which could significantly impair the commission's ability to react nimbly in times of crisis.

Still, markets have a way of correcting themselves, "not right away, but over time." However, damage done in the interim could be significant.

The SEC's focus should be in making markets work for the American people, specifically for the everyday American.

Crenshaw recommends that the commission should encourage disclosure of fundamentals, real financial metrics of companies. They should also favor long-term investing.

Areas of concern include AI and crypto.

She said she often asks, "What are cryptocurrency prices based on?.. Most trading isn't based on crypto fundamentals." That means much of the trading is speculative. 

Regarding crypto, "we can innovate, but need to have appropriate guardrails in place. And on AI, "we will need to build more rigorous scaffolding around the markets regarding AI."

In discussing private assets, "we need to look at whether robust guardrails are needed in regard to these practices." She specifically pointed to concerns about private equity acquiring large shares of sectors such as residential real estate and accounting firms, giving them ownership of the companies that audit them.

Regarding the SEC's intention to allow semi-annual earnings disclosures, instead of quarterly, investment decisions will be informed by stale data, what the company chooses to disclose, and other factors such as inaccurate reporting and "vibes."

Her ambitions have been to promote a level playing field to allow families to "safely and confidently invest in markets."

"Let us govern with ethics and meaning," she said. "Let us give regulators the tools they need to oversee our markets in a meaningful way."

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: CrowdStrike upgraded to Buy at Freedom Broker, PT raised to $550
data.description:
CrowdStrike (CRWD) was upgraded to Buy from Hold by analysts at Freedom Broker on Thursday, following its solid third quarter print earlier this month. The company beat estimates for both top and bottom lines in the quarter and guided full-year ahead of analysts’ expectations.

The brokerage also raised the price target for the stock to $550, up from $430, anticipating an increase in both gross and operating margins. The updated PT represents a 6% upside from the stock’s close on December 10.

CrowdStrike’s upbeat Q3 results reinforce the resilience of its unified, AI-native security platform model, the brokerage noted.

“The company slightly exceeded expectations on both revenue and earnings, while a record increase in recurring revenue and broader contribution from Next-Gen SIEM, cloud security, identity, and Falcon Flex enhanced visibility into multi-year growth,” analyst Almas Almaganbetov said.

The analyst additionally highlighted that the 22% rise in revenue, which surpassed the upper end of its guidance, was driven by CrowdStrike’s fundamental strength rather than one-time factors- specifically, the broadening of the Falcon platform subscription business and Annual Recurring Revenue (ARR) growth.

They believe that the firm’s record net new ARR of $265 million signals strong momentum for the future and a total ARR growth of 23% Y/Y sets a solid foundation for revenue growth through FY2026-2027.

“At the product level, CrowdStrike is successfully diversifying its growth vectors, reducing reliance on any single driver. Next-Gen SIEM delivered a record quarter in net new ARR, actively displacing legacy SIEM platforms by leveraging existing EDR data and aggressive pricing with superior time-to-value,” Almaganbetov wrote.
